title: Literals and formatted I/O types

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Literals and formatted I/O types; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A size_t value is printed with %zu rather than guessing that it has the same type as unsigned long on every target. Your goal is to repair mismatched literals and formatted I/O conversions without invoking undefined behavior. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Translation unit and main; pf state expressions; Scalar types and declarations. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: Literal spelling and suffix select a candidate type, while formatted input and output require a conversion specification compatible with the actual argument pointer or promoted value type. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Integer literal bases change candidate lists, variadic arguments undergo promotions, and mismatched printf or scanf specifications can produce undefined behavior. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Any integer can safely be printed with %d because printf converts numeric argument types automatically.

includes: Match literal forms, suffixes and format specifiers to types.
Repair mismatched literals and formatted I/O conversions without invoking undefined behavior.

outcomes: Repair mismatched literals and formatted I/O conversions without invoking undefined behavior.

examples.title: Literals and formatted I/O types: defined case
examples.situation: A size_t value is printed with %zu rather than guessing that it has the same type as unsigned long on every target.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A size_t value is printed with %zu rather than guessing that it has the same type as unsigned long on every target.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Match each format conversion to the promoted argument type; use %zu for size_t and standard format macros where fixed-width types require them.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Match each format conversion to the promoted argument type; use %zu for size_t and standard format macros where fixed-width types require them.

examples.title: Literals and formatted I/O types: boundary case
examples.situation: A uint64_t value uses the matching PRIu64 macro from inttypes.h when portable fixed-width formatting is required.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A uint64_t value uses the matching PRIu64 macro from inttypes.h when portable fixed-width formatting is required.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Integer literal bases change candidate lists, variadic arguments undergo promotions, and mismatched printf or scanf specifications can produce undefined behavior.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Any integer can safely be printed with %d because printf converts numeric argument types automatically.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Repair mismatched literals and formatted I/O conversions without invoking undefined behavior.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Any integer can safely be printed with %d because printf converts numeric argument types automatically.
misconceptions.correction: Match each format conversion to the promoted argument type; use %zu for size_t and standard format macros where fixed-width types require them.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: Any integer can safely be printed with %d because printf converts numeric argument types automatically.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Literals and formatted I/O types: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
